course_info.title: Advanced Network Security
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Threat Detection with Machine Learning
learning_objectives: Apply machine learning techniques to network anomaly detection
Evaluate ML-based IDS effectiveness and limitations
Implement a behavioral network baseline with automated deviation alerts
readings: Academic papers on ML-based network intrusion detection

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional signature-based detection cannot keep pace with novel threats. Machine learning enables detection of previously unseen attacks by learning normal network behavior and identifying deviations.
key_concepts: Supervised vs unsupervised ML for network security
Feature engineering from network flow data
Anomaly detection: isolation forests, autoencoders
Classification: random forests for traffic categorization
False positive management in ML-based detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Training an isolation forest model on NetFlow data to detect anomalous network behavior, then testing against simulated attack traffic

review.value: Unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm that isolates observations by randomly selecting features and split values. Anomalies require fewer splits to isolate, yielding a shorter path length.
Extracting meaningful features from raw network data: flow duration, packet count, byte count, inter-arrival times, port entropy, protocol ratios

learning_objectives: Analyze the security implications of SDN architecture
Identify SDN-specific attack vectors and defenses

lecture_notes.introduction: SDN separates the control plane from the data plane, enabling programmable network security. This creates both powerful defense capabilities and new attack surfaces.
key_concepts: SDN architecture: controller, southbound API, northbound API
OpenFlow protocol and flow table management
Micro-segmentation: per-workload firewall policies via SDN
SDN attacks: controller compromise, flow table poisoning, DoS of control plane
Network Function Virtualization (NFV) for security services
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using an OpenDaylight controller to implement micro-segmentation policies and demonstrating how flow rules dynamically isolate a compromised host

review.value: The controller is the single point of control (and failure). Compromise gives the attacker control over all network flows. Must be hardened, redundant, and monitored.
SDN enables firewall policies at the individual VM/container level, not just at network boundaries. Dramatically limits lateral movement after a breach.

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Persistent Threat Detection and Hunting
learning_objectives: Characterize APT tactics, techniques, and procedures
Develop threat hunting hypotheses based on MITRE ATT&CK
Implement detection engineering for APT techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Advanced Persistent Threats operate with nation-state resources and patience. Detecting them requires going beyond automated alerts to proactive, intelligence-driven hunting.
key_concepts: APT lifecycle: initial access, execution, persistence, privilege escalation, lateral movement, exfiltration
MITRE ATT&CK-based hunting methodology
Network-based APT indicators: beaconing patterns, DNS anomalies, encrypted C2
Hunting for lateral movement: anomalous SMB, RDP, WMI traffic
Threat intelligence integration for IOC-based detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hunting for APT-style beaconing in NetFlow data using JA3 fingerprints and timing analysis to identify encrypted C2 channels

review.value: APT malware regularly contacts C2 servers at predictable intervals. Detecting beaconing: analyze connection timing patterns using standard deviation of inter-connection intervals. Low jitter = beaconing.
MD5 hash of TLS ClientHello parameters (cipher suites, extensions, curves). Each TLS client produces a unique fingerprint. Known malware JA3 hashes enable detection regardless of destination.

learning_objectives: Analyze encrypted traffic without decryption using metadata
Implement TLS inspection and its privacy implications
Use traffic analysis techniques to classify encrypted flows

lecture_notes.introduction: With over 90% of web traffic now encrypted, defenders can no longer rely on deep packet inspection. Encrypted traffic analysis uses metadata and behavioral patterns to detect threats without decryption.
key_concepts: TLS metadata: certificate info, SNI, JA3/JA3S fingerprints
Flow-based analysis: packet sizes, timing, directionality
Machine learning on encrypted traffic: classifying applications and malware
TLS inspection (man-in-the-middle proxy): capabilities and privacy concerns
DNS-over-HTTPS and DNS-over-TLS impact on visibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Classifying encrypted traffic flows using packet size distributions and timing patterns to distinguish between legitimate HTTPS and malware C2

review.value: Even encrypted traffic reveals: who talks to whom, when, how much data, certificate details, TLS parameters. This metadata alone can identify threats without breaking encryption.
ML models trained on packet size distributions and inter-arrival times can classify encrypted traffic (web browsing, streaming, file transfer, malware C2) with high accuracy.

learning_objectives: Deploy honeypots and honeynets for threat intelligence
Implement network deception with decoy assets
Design an active defense strategy within legal boundaries
readings: The Art of Intrusion Detection Ch. 5-8

lecture_notes.introduction: Active defense shifts from purely reactive security to strategically engaging with adversaries using deception, misdirection, and deliberate engagement to gather intelligence.
key_concepts: Honeypots: low-interaction (Cowrie, Dionaea) vs high-interaction
Honeynets: networks of honeypots simulating real infrastructure
Honey tokens: fake credentials, documents, and database records
Canary tokens: tripwires that alert when accessed
Legal and ethical boundaries of active defense
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploying Cowrie SSH honeypot, generating canary tokens, and analyzing attacker behavior captured by the honeypot

review.value: Any interaction with a honeypot is suspicious by definition (legitimate users have no reason to access it). Provides high-fidelity alerts with zero false positives for early breach detection.
Files (Word docs, PDFs) or credentials that trigger an alert when opened or used. Plant in file shares, admin directories, or credential stores. Any access indicates compromise.

learning_objectives: Implement SOAR workflows for common alert types
Integrate security tools through APIs and automation

lecture_notes.introduction: Security teams are overwhelmed by alert volume. SOAR platforms automate repetitive response tasks, enabling analysts to focus on complex investigations while routine incidents are handled automatically.
key_concepts: SOAR architecture: orchestration, automation, response, case management
Playbook design: trigger conditions, decision trees, automated actions
API integration: SIEM, firewall, EDR, threat intelligence
Automated enrichment: IP reputation, domain age, hash lookup
Human-in-the-loop decisions for high-impact actions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a SOAR playbook that automatically triages phishing alerts: extracts IOCs, checks reputation, blocks malicious URLs, and escalates to an analyst if uncertain
setup_requirements: Shuffle SOAR platform or TheHive + Cortex

review.value: Define trigger (SIEM alert), enrichment steps (lookup IOCs), decision points (malicious threshold), automated actions (block IP, disable account), and escalation criteria (analyst review).
SOAR reduces Mean Time to Respond from hours to minutes for common incident types. Automated containment of confirmed threats while analysts handle ambiguous cases.

quizzes.topic: ML Detection, SDN Security, and APT Hunting

questions.question: You are analyzing NetFlow data and notice that host 10.0.5.22 has been connecting to IP 198.51.100.42:443 every 300 seconds (+/- 5 seconds) for the past 72 hours, transferring approximately 2KB per connection. Explain what this traffic pattern suggests, what additional data points you would analyze to confirm, and how you would determine if it is malicious.
questions.answer: The regular 300-second interval with minimal jitter strongly suggests automated beaconing, consistent with C2 communication. The small, consistent data size suggests check-in/heartbeat traffic rather than data exfiltration. Additional analysis: (1) JA3 fingerprint of the TLS client to compare against known malware fingerprints. (2) Certificate analysis: is the certificate self-signed, recently issued, or from a free CA? (3) Domain/IP reputation lookup. (4) Historical first-seen date: new connections to previously uncontacted IPs are suspicious. (5) Check if 10.0.5.22 connects to any other unusual IPs. To confirm malicious: isolate the host (without alerting), capture memory for forensic analysis, and examine the process making the connections.

questions.question: Explain why an SDN controller is both a powerful security tool and a critical risk. Describe two security architectures that mitigate the risk of SDN controller compromise.
questions.answer: Power: The SDN controller has a global view of the entire network and can programmatically modify any flow rule instantly. This enables real-time micro-segmentation, automated quarantine, and dynamic policy enforcement. Risk: Controller compromise gives the attacker god-mode over the network: redirect traffic, disable security rules, create covert channels, or cause total network outage. Architecture 1: Controller clustering with Byzantine fault tolerance. Deploy 3+ controllers where flow rule changes require consensus. Even if one controller is compromised, the others reject malicious rules. Architecture 2: Separation of security policy controllers from routing controllers. A dedicated security controller enforces firewall rules with its own authentication, separate from the routing controller. Compromise of the routing controller does not bypass security policies.

quizzes.topic: Encrypted Traffic Analysis, Deception, and SOAR

questions.question: A security architect argues that TLS inspection (MITM proxy) should be deployed on all network traffic for security visibility. Present two strong arguments for and two against this approach, considering both security and organizational factors.
questions.answer: For: (1) Restores visibility into 90%+ of network traffic, enabling DLP, malware detection, and compliance monitoring that is otherwise blind to encrypted content. (2) Enables IDS signatures and content filtering to detect threats that would bypass perimeter controls inside encrypted channels. Against: (1) Breaks end-to-end encryption, creating a single point of decryption where all traffic is exposed. If the proxy is compromised, all traffic is compromised. Also breaks certificate pinning for applications that implement it. (2) Privacy and legal concerns: intercepting encrypted traffic may violate employee privacy expectations, healthcare (HIPAA) and financial regulations, and may be illegal in some jurisdictions without explicit consent. Banking and healthcare sites should be exempted.

rubric.key: Two valid arguments for TLS inspection
Consideration of both security and organizational factors

questions.question: Design a SOAR playbook for automatically responding to a 'brute force SSH login' SIEM alert. Include the trigger condition, at least four automated steps, one decision point requiring human judgment, and the expected outcome.
questions.answer: Trigger: SIEM alert when >10 failed SSH login attempts from the same source IP within 5 minutes. Step 1: Enrich the source IP (geolocation, reputation lookup via VirusTotal/AbuseIPDB, check if it is a known VPN/Tor exit node). Step 2: Query SIEM for this IP's historical activity across all systems (has it targeted other hosts?). Step 3: Check if any of the attempted usernames correspond to actual system accounts. Step 4: If the IP is external and reputation is malicious, automatically create a firewall block rule for the source IP (24-hour temporary block). Decision point: If the IP is internal or belongs to a known partner, escalate to an analyst for investigation (could be a misconfigured service, compromised internal host, or legitimate user lockout). Step 5: Create an incident ticket with all enrichment data. Expected outcome: External brute force blocked within 2 minutes of detection. Internal/partner sources investigated by an analyst with full context pre-gathered.

major_assignments.title: Advanced Network Defense Architecture
major_assignments.description: Design and implement an advanced network defense architecture incorporating ML-based detection, deception technologies, and automated response for a provided enterprise scenario.
objectives: Deploy ML-based anomaly detection on network traffic
Implement deception technologies for early breach detection
Build automated response playbooks for detected threats
Demonstrate detection of simulated APT-style attacks
deliverables: Architecture design document with defense-in-depth justification
Working ML detection model with training and testing results
Deployed honeypots and canary tokens with monitoring
SOAR playbook for automated threat response

criteria: Appropriate ML algorithm selection with justification
Model training with proper train/test split
Honeypots deployed and capturing attacker behavior
Canary tokens placed in strategic locations
Alerts generated on deception asset interaction
SOAR playbook functional for detected threats
Comprehensive defense architecture integrating multiple advanced techniques
Demonstrated detection capability against realistic attack scenarios
Professional documentation suitable for enterprise deployment

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of advanced network security concepts and techniques

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs with ML detection, deception, and SOAR automation

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: detect and respond to simulated network attacks

final_project.description: Capstone: defend a network against a multi-phase simulated APT

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. Reports and code must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All detection models and automation must be your own work. Using pre-built models without modification and analysis is not acceptable.
course_policies.lab_safety: All testing must be conducted within the isolated lab environment. ML models must be trained on provided datasets only.
